key_moments.content: User admitted she has been working 12-hour days for 3 months and has not told anyone, including family
User described her manager publicly criticising her work in a team meeting, leaving her feeling humiliated and questioning her competence
User revealed she skipped lunch every day this week and has been having headaches she has been ignoring
User said she cried in the bathroom at work twice this week but made sure nobody saw her because she does not want to appear weak
User expressed a desire to stop feeling like a fraud at work and to be able to leave the office before 8pm at least twice a week

unresolved_themes: fear of being perceived as incompetent
user_commitments: Will try to take a 10-minute walk outside at lunch at least once this week

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Welcome, Aisha. I'm glad you're here. There's no pressure to cover anything specific today — this is your space. What's been on your mind lately?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I'm not even sure where to start, honestly. Work, I guess. It's just... a lot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: That's a perfectly fine place to start. When you say it's a lot — what does that actually look like in your day?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I've been staying until like 8 or 9pm every night. For months. I tell my family I'm 'just finishing something up' but it's been like this since October. My manager keeps piling things on and I just... say yes. Every time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: That takes a real toll. Three months is a long time to be carrying something alone. What happens when you think about saying no to your manager?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: It feels impossible. Last week he called out my presentation in front of the whole team. Said it lacked 'strategic thinking.' In front of everyone. I wanted to disappear.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: That sounds incredibly painful — to be criticised in public like that. What went through your mind in that moment?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: That maybe he's right. That I'm not actually good at this job and everyone is slowly figuring it out. I've been waiting for someone to tell me I don't belong there.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: I want to sit with that for a second. You said you've been waiting for that. How long have you felt that way?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Honestly? Since I got hired. Two years. I've just been holding my breath.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Two years is a long time to hold your breath. I'm also noticing — you mentioned working 12-hour days, your manager publicly criticised you. How are you taking care of yourself physically in all of this?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I mean... I'm fine. I skipped lunch a few times this week. I've had headaches but that's probably just the screen time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: A few times or every day this week?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: ...Every day. And I cried in the bathroom yesterday. And on Tuesday. But I made sure nobody saw.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: I'm really glad you told me that. That took courage. The fact that you're making sure nobody sees — what does it mean to you if someone does see?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: That I'm weak. That I can't handle it. That they were right about me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: We're going to come back to that belief — because I think it's doing a lot of heavy lifting in your life right now. For today, I want to ask: what would feeling even 10% better look like at work? Not fixed — just 10% better.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Leaving before 8pm? Not feeling sick when I open my laptop in the morning. Not crying at work.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Those are real and valid things to work toward. Let's start small. Could you commit to one thing this week — just one? Like stepping outside for ten minutes at lunch?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I can try that. It feels stupid but I can try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: It's not stupid at all. It's a start. And starting is the hardest part. You did something harder today — you showed up and talked about things you've been holding alone for a long time. That matters.
